1→# Enrichment Review: finite_math (MeetFin + FinNavigation)
     2→
     3→Only MeetFin (W01, 20 levels) and FinNavigation (W02, 9 levels) have been authored. The remaining 27 worlds listed have no level files. This review covers the two existing worlds.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Ranked Suggestions
     8→
     9→### 1. Fin.eta / reconstruction theorem is used but never named
    10→
    11→**What**: The pattern "if you know i.val = k, then i = ⟨k, proof⟩" appears in L09 (FinEta) and L10 (ProofIrrelevance) but is never given a name or connected to the general subtype eta principle (`Subtype.eta` / `Fin.eta`).
    12→
    13→**Why**: This is one of the most important structural facts about subtypes — that an element equals its own reconstruction from components. Naming it teaches the learner to recognize the pattern across different subtypes later. The level title is "The Round-Trip Property" (L09), which is fine, but the *conclusion* should mention that this has a name in Lean (`Fin.eta`) and that it's a special case of `Subtype.eta`. Currently the conclusion says "the round-trip principle" with no link to the library name.
    14→
    15→**Where**: L09 conclusion text, L10 conclusion text. No new level needed.
    16→
    17→**Effort**: Low (add a sentence to two conclusions)
    18→
    19→**Recommend**: Yes
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→### 2. Missing: castSucc injectivity
    24→
    25→**What**: FinNavigation proves succ injectivity (Boss Part 2) but never states or proves castSucc injectivity. Since castSucc preserves values, castSucc is trivially injective — and the proof is a one-liner with the same pattern (ext_iff + val_castSucc + omega).
    26→
    27→**Why**: The decomposition (Boss Part 1) implicitly relies on both functions being injective. succ injectivity is proved; castSucc injectivity is not. This is an equivalence that goes unmentioned: the learner proves one half but not the other. More importantly, castSucc injectivity is needed later when working with Finset.map (which requires an embedding = injective function), so establishing it here seeds vocabulary for W07.
    28→
    29→**Where**: New level in FinNavigation (between L05 and L06, or as a Boss Part 3), or modify existing Boss to include it.
    30→
    31→**Effort**: Medium (add one level or extend the boss)
    32→
    33→**Recommend**: Yes
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→### 3. No concrete application of Fin to motivate the abstraction
    38→
    39→**What**: Both worlds work entirely with Fin as a self-contained mathematical object. No level shows Fin being *used* for something — indexing a vector, representing a permutation, labeling vertices of a polygon, etc.
    40→
    41→**Why**: The MeetFin introduction mentions "indices of vectors, labels for vertices of a polygon, positions in a sequence" but no level ever instantiates this. The world teaches *how* Fin works but not *why you would use it*. A single concrete "application level" — e.g., "define the vertices of a triangle as elements of Fin 3, prove they're all distinct" or "index a 3-tuple of natural numbers" — would ground the abstraction. This is especially important because the learner profile says "no assumed familiarity with finite set theory." Without a concrete anchor, Fin feels like an arbitrary exercise.
    42→
    43→**Where**: MeetFin, after L17 (case analysis practice) and before L18 (inequality). A level that uses Fin 3 to index something concrete, combining construction (L01), extraction (L02), and case analysis (L16-17).
    44→
    45→**Effort**: High (new level)
    46→
    47→**Recommend**: Yes
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→### 4. The "intro h; cases h" discrimination pattern deserves a named strategy
    52→
    53→**What**: L18 introduces `intro h; cases h` for proving inequality of Fin elements, but this is actually a general-purpose discrimination pattern for any type with distinct constructors (Nat, Bool, Fin, etc.). The conclusion mentions this but doesn't give it a name or connect it to the concept of "discriminability" / "injection + discrimination."
    54→
    55→**Why**: This pattern is used again in the Boss (L20 Part 2) and will recur throughout the course whenever the learner needs to prove two concrete terms are distinct. Naming the strategy — "constructor discrimination" or "injection-discrimination" — would help the learner recognize when to apply it. The conclusion of L18 says "This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss" but doesn't name the concept.
    56→
    57→**Where**: L18 conclusion text. No new level needed.
    58→
    59→**Effort**: Low (expand conclusion by 2-3 sentences)
    60→
    61→**Recommend**: Consider
    62→
    63→---
    64→
    65→### 5. Missing converse of the decomposition: castSucc images are exactly the non-last elements
    66→
    67→**What**: FinNavigation Boss Part 1 proves that every element of Fin (n+1) is either last or a castSucc image. But the converse — that these two cases are *exclusive* (proved in L06: castSucc never reaches last) — is never explicitly combined with the exhaustion to state the full decomposition as a disjoint union.
    68→
    69→**Why**: The separation (L06) and the exhaustion (Boss Part 1) are proved in separate places. The conclusion of L06 says "the images of castSucc and Fin.last don't overlap" and the Boss conclusion says the decomposition exists. But nowhere is the full statement articulated: "Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) ⊔ {last}, disjointly." This is the structural fact that will power Fin.cons/Fin.snoc in the next world. Stating it explicitly — even just in prose — would help the learner see the pieces fit together.
    70→
    71→**Where**: FinNavigation Boss conclusion. No new level needed.
    72→
    73→**Effort**: Low (add a paragraph to the Boss conclusion connecting L06 and Boss Part 1)
    74→
    75→**Recommend**: Yes
    76→
    77→---
    78→
    79→### 6. Fin 2 ↔ Bool equivalence is mentioned but never formalized
    80→
    81→**What**: L16 conclusion says "Fun fact: Fin 2 is equivalent to Bool — both have exactly two elements." This is stated as a throwaway comment. The equivalence is mathematically significant and could be a level.
    82→
    83→**Why**: Fin 2 ↔ Bool is the simplest example of a type equivalence and foreshadows the Fintype world (W11), where the learner will see that every type with n elements is equivalent to Fin n. Making this a level — even a simple one where the learner constructs the bijection — would plant the seed early and make the Fintype world feel like a generalization rather than a new idea. It also concretely demonstrates *why* Fin is the canonical finite type: it's the "standard model."
    84→
    85→**Where**: New level in MeetFin after L16, or in PsetFin (W04).
    86→
    87→**Effort**: High (new level with non-trivial statement)
    88→
    89→**Recommend**: Consider
    90→
    91→---
    92→
    93→### 7. Proof irrelevance (L10) should mention that this is NOT special to Fin
    94→
    95→**What**: L10 conclusion says "This isn't special to Fin — it applies to every subtype in Lean." But this connection is made in one sentence at the end. The fact that proof irrelevance is a property of *all* subtypes (and indeed of Lean's type theory via `Subsingleton (α → Prop)`) is a deep insight that deserves more than a passing mention.
    96→
    97→**Why**: The learner will encounter other subtypes — `{ n : ℕ // n.Prime }`, `{ s : Finset α // s.Nonempty }`, etc. — and needs to know that the same ext_iff + omega pattern works for all of them. Emphasizing this in the conclusion (or adding a follow-up remark) helps the learner generalize.
    98→
    99→**Where**: L10 conclusion. No new level needed.
   100→
   101→**Effort**: Low (expand conclusion by 2-3 sentences)
   102→
   103→**Recommend**: Consider
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→### 8. FinNavigation never addresses Fin.zero / Fin.castSucc 0
   108→
   109→**What**: `Fin.castSucc (0 : Fin n) : Fin (n+1)` has value 0, the *minimum* element. This is the dual of `Fin.last n` (the maximum). Neither world introduces `(0 : Fin (n+1))` as a named landmark or discusses the minimum.
   110→
   111→**Why**: The symmetry between "minimum = 0 = castSucc 0" and "maximum = last" is a natural pairing. Having it explicit would strengthen the navigation toolkit. It also matters practically: when the learner builds tuples with Fin.cons in W03, the first element is accessed at index 0, which is `castSucc 0` when thinking about the decomposition.
   112→
   113→**Where**: FinNavigation, between L03 (castSucc) and L04 (succ). A brief level or a remark in L03's conclusion.
   114→
   115→**Effort**: Low (remark in conclusion) to Medium (new level)
   116→
   117→**Recommend**: Consider
   118→
   119→---
   120→
   121→### 9. omega's capabilities are under-documented for the learner
   122→
   123→**What**: `omega` is introduced in L01 and used in nearly every level thereafter, but its documentation (TacticDoc in L01) only says it handles "linear arithmetic over ℕ and ℤ" and gives one example. It doesn't explain that omega also closes goals from contradictory hypotheses (used starting L03), handles `≤`/`<`/`=`/`≠` simultaneously, or works across multiple hypotheses.
   124→
   125→**Why**: The learner sees omega close wildly different kinds of goals — `3 < 5`, contradiction from `x.val = 5 ∧ x.val < 5`, and `i.val ≤ 4` from `i.val < 5` — without a clear explanation of why one tactic handles all of these. A richer TacticDoc or a "what omega can do" remark in one of the early conclusions would reduce the feeling that omega is magic.
   126→
   127→**Where**: L03 conclusion (first time omega closes a contradiction) or update the TacticDoc.
   128→
   129→**Effort**: Low (expand TacticDoc or add a sentence to L03 conclusion)
   130→
   131→**Recommend**: Yes
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→### 10. Missing: what happens when you apply succ to the last element?
   136→
   137→**What**: FinNavigation teaches `succ : Fin n → Fin (n+1)`. The learner might wonder: can you compose succ to "walk" through all of Fin (n+1)? What is `(Fin.last n).succ`? This is never addressed.
   138→
   139→**Why**: `Fin.last` lives in `Fin (n+1)`, and `Fin.succ` takes `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, so `(Fin.last n).succ` is not directly expressible (type mismatch: `last n : Fin (n+1)`, not `Fin n`). This is a natural "what if?" question that would help the learner understand the type signatures more deeply. A remark in L04's conclusion would suffice.
   140→
   141→**Where**: L04 conclusion (after introducing succ).
   142→
   143→**Effort**: Low (add a "what if?" remark)
   144→
   145→**Recommend**: Consider
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→## Overall Impression
   150→
   151→Both worlds are pedagogically strong. MeetFin is thorough in its 20-level coverage of Fin construction, extraction, equality, edge cases, case analysis, and inequality — the pacing is good, each level isolates one lesson, and the boss genuinely integrates prior moves. FinNavigation is tighter at 9 levels but covers its three navigation functions and the decomposition cleanly.
   152→
   153→The single most important improvement is **suggestion #1 (naming the eta/reconstruction pattern)**. The pattern "if you know the value, you can identify the element" is used repeatedly across both worlds and will recur throughout the course, but it currently has no name. Giving it the library name (`Fin.eta`) and connecting it to the general subtype principle would be a low-effort, high-impact improvement that strengthens transfer to later worlds.
   154→

# Enrichment Review: finite_math (MeetFin + FinNavigation)

Only MeetFin (W01, 20 levels) and FinNavigation (W02, 9 levels) have been authored. The remaining 27 worlds listed have no level files. This review covers the two existing worlds.

---

## Ranked Suggestions

### 1. Fin.eta / reconstruction theorem is used but never named

**What**: The pattern "if you know i.val = k, then i = ⟨k, proof⟩" appears in L09 (FinEta) and L10 (ProofIrrelevance) but is never given a name or connected to the general subtype eta principle (`Subtype.eta` / `Fin.eta`).

**Why**: This is one of the most important structural facts about subtypes — that an element equals its own reconstruction from components. Naming it teaches the learner to recognize the pattern across different subtypes later. The level title is "The Round-Trip Property" (L09), which is fine, but the *conclusion* should mention that this has a name in Lean (`Fin.eta`) and that it's a special case of `Subtype.eta`. Currently the conclusion says "the round-trip principle" with no link to the library name.

**Where**: L09 conclusion text, L10 conclusion text. No new level needed.

**Effort**: Low (add a sentence to two conclusions)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 2. Missing: castSucc injectivity

**What**: FinNavigation proves succ injectivity (Boss Part 2) but never states or proves castSucc injectivity. Since castSucc preserves values, castSucc is trivially injective — and the proof is a one-liner with the same pattern (ext_iff + val_castSucc + omega).

**Why**: The decomposition (Boss Part 1) implicitly relies on both functions being injective. succ injectivity is proved; castSucc injectivity is not. This is an equivalence that goes unmentioned: the learner proves one half but not the other. More importantly, castSucc injectivity is needed later when working with Finset.map (which requires an embedding = injective function), so establishing it here seeds vocabulary for W07.

**Where**: New level in FinNavigation (between L05 and L06, or as a Boss Part 3), or modify existing Boss to include it.

**Effort**: Medium (add one level or extend the boss)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 3. No concrete application of Fin to motivate the abstraction

**What**: Both worlds work entirely with Fin as a self-contained mathematical object. No level shows Fin being *used* for something — indexing a vector, representing a permutation, labeling vertices of a polygon, etc.

**Why**: The MeetFin introduction mentions "indices of vectors, labels for vertices of a polygon, positions in a sequence" but no level ever instantiates this. The world teaches *how* Fin works but not *why you would use it*. A single concrete "application level" — e.g., "define the vertices of a triangle as elements of Fin 3, prove they're all distinct" or "index a 3-tuple of natural numbers" — would ground the abstraction. This is especially important because the learner profile says "no assumed familiarity with finite set theory." Without a concrete anchor, Fin feels like an arbitrary exercise.

**Where**: MeetFin, after L17 (case analysis practice) and before L18 (inequality). A level that uses Fin 3 to index something concrete, combining construction (L01), extraction (L02), and case analysis (L16-17).

**Effort**: High (new level)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 4. The "intro h; cases h" discrimination pattern deserves a named strategy

**What**: L18 introduces `intro h; cases h` for proving inequality of Fin elements, but this is actually a general-purpose discrimination pattern for any type with distinct constructors (Nat, Bool, Fin, etc.). The conclusion mentions this but doesn't give it a name or connect it to the concept of "discriminability" / "injection + discrimination."

**Why**: This pattern is used again in the Boss (L20 Part 2) and will recur throughout the course whenever the learner needs to prove two concrete terms are distinct. Naming the strategy — "constructor discrimination" or "injection-discrimination" — would help the learner recognize when to apply it. The conclusion of L18 says "This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss" but doesn't name the concept.

**Where**: L18 conclusion text. No new level needed.

**Effort**: Low (expand conclusion by 2-3 sentences)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 5. Missing converse of the decomposition: castSucc images are exactly the non-last elements

**What**: FinNavigation Boss Part 1 proves that every element of Fin (n+1) is either last or a castSucc image. But the converse — that these two cases are *exclusive* (proved in L06: castSucc never reaches last) — is never explicitly combined with the exhaustion to state the full decomposition as a disjoint union.

**Why**: The separation (L06) and the exhaustion (Boss Part 1) are proved in separate places. The conclusion of L06 says "the images of castSucc and Fin.last don't overlap" and the Boss conclusion says the decomposition exists. But nowhere is the full statement articulated: "Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) ⊔ {last}, disjointly." This is the structural fact that will power Fin.cons/Fin.snoc in the next world. Stating it explicitly — even just in prose — would help the learner see the pieces fit together.

**Where**: FinNavigation Boss conclusion. No new level needed.

**Effort**: Low (add a paragraph to the Boss conclusion connecting L06 and Boss Part 1)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 6. Fin 2 ↔ Bool equivalence is mentioned but never formalized

**What**: L16 conclusion says "Fun fact: Fin 2 is equivalent to Bool — both have exactly two elements." This is stated as a throwaway comment. The equivalence is mathematically significant and could be a level.

**Why**: Fin 2 ↔ Bool is the simplest example of a type equivalence and foreshadows the Fintype world (W11), where the learner will see that every type with n elements is equivalent to Fin n. Making this a level — even a simple one where the learner constructs the bijection — would plant the seed early and make the Fintype world feel like a generalization rather than a new idea. It also concretely demonstrates *why* Fin is the canonical finite type: it's the "standard model."

**Where**: New level in MeetFin after L16, or in PsetFin (W04).

**Effort**: High (new level with non-trivial statement)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 7. Proof irrelevance (L10) should mention that this is NOT special to Fin

**What**: L10 conclusion says "This isn't special to Fin — it applies to every subtype in Lean." But this connection is made in one sentence at the end. The fact that proof irrelevance is a property of *all* subtypes (and indeed of Lean's type theory via `Subsingleton (α → Prop)`) is a deep insight that deserves more than a passing mention.

**Why**: The learner will encounter other subtypes — `{ n : ℕ // n.Prime }`, `{ s : Finset α // s.Nonempty }`, etc. — and needs to know that the same ext_iff + omega pattern works for all of them. Emphasizing this in the conclusion (or adding a follow-up remark) helps the learner generalize.

**Where**: L10 conclusion. No new level needed.

**Effort**: Low (expand conclusion by 2-3 sentences)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 8. FinNavigation never addresses Fin.zero / Fin.castSucc 0

**What**: `Fin.castSucc (0 : Fin n) : Fin (n+1)` has value 0, the *minimum* element. This is the dual of `Fin.last n` (the maximum). Neither world introduces `(0 : Fin (n+1))` as a named landmark or discusses the minimum.

**Why**: The symmetry between "minimum = 0 = castSucc 0" and "maximum = last" is a natural pairing. Having it explicit would strengthen the navigation toolkit. It also matters practically: when the learner builds tuples with Fin.cons in W03, the first element is accessed at index 0, which is `castSucc 0` when thinking about the decomposition.

**Where**: FinNavigation, between L03 (castSucc) and L04 (succ). A brief level or a remark in L03's conclusion.

**Effort**: Low (remark in conclusion) to Medium (new level)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 9. omega's capabilities are under-documented for the learner

**What**: `omega` is introduced in L01 and used in nearly every level thereafter, but its documentation (TacticDoc in L01) only says it handles "linear arithmetic over ℕ and ℤ" and gives one example. It doesn't explain that omega also closes goals from contradictory hypotheses (used starting L03), handles `≤`/`<`/`=`/`≠` simultaneously, or works across multiple hypotheses.

**Why**: The learner sees omega close wildly different kinds of goals — `3 < 5`, contradiction from `x.val = 5 ∧ x.val < 5`, and `i.val ≤ 4` from `i.val < 5` — without a clear explanation of why one tactic handles all of these. A richer TacticDoc or a "what omega can do" remark in one of the early conclusions would reduce the feeling that omega is magic.

**Where**: L03 conclusion (first time omega closes a contradiction) or update the TacticDoc.

**Effort**: Low (expand TacticDoc or add a sentence to L03 conclusion)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 10. Missing: what happens when you apply succ to the last element?

**What**: FinNavigation teaches `succ : Fin n → Fin (n+1)`. The learner might wonder: can you compose succ to "walk" through all of Fin (n+1)? What is `(Fin.last n).succ`? This is never addressed.

**Why**: `Fin.last` lives in `Fin (n+1)`, and `Fin.succ` takes `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, so `(Fin.last n).succ` is not directly expressible (type mismatch: `last n : Fin (n+1)`, not `Fin n`). This is a natural "what if?" question that would help the learner understand the type signatures more deeply. A remark in L04's conclusion would suffice.

**Where**: L04 conclusion (after introducing succ).

**Effort**: Low (add a "what if?" remark)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

## Overall Impression

Both worlds are pedagogically strong. MeetFin is thorough in its 20-level coverage of Fin construction, extraction, equality, edge cases, case analysis, and inequality — the pacing is good, each level isolates one lesson, and the boss genuinely integrates prior moves. FinNavigation is tighter at 9 levels but covers its three navigation functions and the decomposition cleanly.

The single most important improvement is **suggestion #1 (naming the eta/reconstruction pattern)**. The pattern "if you know the value, you can identify the element" is used repeatedly across both worlds and will recur throughout the course, but it currently has no name. Giving it the library name (`Fin.eta`) and connecting it to the general subtype principle would be a low-effort, high-impact improvement that strengthens transfer to later worlds.

# Playtest Audit: MeetFin + FinNavigation

**Course**: finite_math
**Worlds audited**: MeetFin (20 levels), FinNavigation (9 levels)
**Worlds NOT yet built**: FinTuples, PsetFin, FinsetBasics, FinsetOperations, FinsetImage, PsetFinset, Cardinality, AbstractionLadder, Fintype, CountingTypes, PsetCardinality, BigOpIntro, BigOpAlgebra, FinsetInduction, SummationFormulas, PsetBigOp, BinomialCoefficients, Powerset, BinomialTheorem, PascalsTriangle, PsetCombinatorics, Products, Finsupp, Matrices, CountingTechniques, PsetCounting, Finale
**Build**: Passes (exit code 0)

---

## 1. Overall Verdict

**FAIL** — One P0 defect (MeetFin L15: omega bypasses destructuring lesson).

Both worlds are well-designed with strong coverage, clean inventory, and good hint layering. The single P0 is a narrow exploitability issue with a straightforward fix.

---

## 2. Rubric Scores

| Category | Score | Notes |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Coverage closure | 4 | Every core item has intro + support + retrieval + integration in boss |
| Granularity fit | 4 | Each level has one dominant lesson; worlds are coherently centered |
| Proof-move teaching | 4 | Proof shapes are made visible and generalizable (Fin equality pattern, contradiction pattern, case analysis pattern, rewrite-to-values-then-omega) |
| Inventory design | 4 | Items unlocked at right time; all documented; disabled theorems well-chosen (Unique.eq_default, Subsingleton.elim, Fin.forall_fin_two, Fin.succ_inj, etc.); TheoremTab "Fin" used |
| Hint design and recoverability | 3 | Hints are well-layered (visible strategy + hidden code). But L15's bypass means the hint pathway never fires for a learner who uses omega. Boss hints DO rescue this gap. |
| Worked example -> practice -> transfer -> boss | 3 | Good progression. Minor gap: `ext` tactic introduced in L14, not practiced again until Boss L20 (mitigated by extensive `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` experience). |
| Mathematical authenticity | 4 | Clear mathematical stakes throughout. Fin 0 emptiness, proof irrelevance, explosion principle, the last-or-castSucc decomposition are all mathematically meaningful with good connections to paper math. |
| Paper-proof transfer | 4 | Conclusions consistently translate Lean moves to mathematical language. Summary tables map notation to meaning. Decomposition connected to "every k <= n either equals n or is < n". |
| Technical fit and maintainability | 4 | Clean build. Sane dependencies. Coherent center of gravity. Easy to extend. |

**Average**: 3.78
**Minimum**: 3

---

## 3. P0 Defects (blocking)

### P0-1: MeetFin L15 — omega bypasses destructuring lesson

**File**: `MeetFin/L15_DestructuringFin.lean`
**Statement**: `(x : Fin 3) : x.val ≤ 2`
**Dominant lesson**: Learning `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` syntax
**Exploit**: `omega` closes the goal in one step. omega automatically sees `x.isLt : x.val < 3` from the type of `x` and derives `x.val ≤ 2`. No destructuring needed.
**Verified**: `example (x : Fin 3) : x.val ≤ 2 := by omega` compiles.

The entire purpose of this level is preparation for L16-L17 case analysis. If bypassed, the learner hits L16 without having practiced destructuring syntax. L16's hints DO teach destructuring from scratch, providing a recovery path, but this is not how the progression should work.

**Fix options** (ranked):
1. **Best**: Change the statement to one that requires destructuring. E.g., `Statement (x : Fin 3) : ∃ (v : ℕ), v < 3 ∧ x = ⟨v, by omega⟩` — after `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`, the learner can `exact ⟨v, hlt, rfl⟩`. This can't be omega'd.
2. **Simpler**: Add `omega` to `DisabledTactic` for L15 only. Then the intended proof `cases x with | mk v hlt => exact Nat.le_of_lt_succ hlt` works, but introduces `Nat.le_of_lt_succ` (untaught). Alternatively use `exact Nat.lt_succ_iff.mp hlt`.
3. **Simplest**: Change the goal to `x.val < 3` so the proof after destructuring is just `exact hlt`. But this is also closable by `exact x.isLt` without destructuring.

Recommendation: Option 1 — it forces the syntax lesson and omega can't bypass it.

---

## 4. P1 Defects (high priority)

None.

---

## 5. P2 Defects (medium)

### P2-1: FinNavigation L01/L03/L04 — rfl closes val lemma introduction goals

**Files**: `L01_TheLastElement.lean`, `L03_EmbeddingCastSucc.lean`, `L04_MovingForwardSucc.lean`
**Exploit**: `rfl` closes:
- `(Fin.last 4).val = 4`
- `∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val`
- `∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1`

These are definitionally true. L01 already acknowledges this ("Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here"). The named lemmas (`val_last`, `val_castSucc`, `val_succ`) are essential in later levels (L05+) where they're used as rewrite tools inside larger proofs — omega cannot bypass those. So the risk is low: a learner who uses `rfl` here will still need the lemma names later.

**No fix needed**, but L03/L04 could add a parenthetical like L01 does to acknowledge that `rfl` works.

### P2-2: MeetFin L14 — ext tactic introduced with no practice before boss

**File**: `L14_UniqueElement.lean` introduces `ext`. Next use is Boss L20.
**Issue**: No supported practice level between introduction and integration under pressure.
**Mitigation**: The learner has extensive experience with `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` (L05-L10), and `ext` is presented as shorthand for it. The mental model is already in place.
**Fix**: Not urgent. A future enrichment pass could add a practice level between L14 and L20 using `ext` on a fresh surface form.

### P2-3: MeetFin L03/L11/L12/L13 — omega closes goals without explicit isLt extraction

**Files**: Multiple levels where the intended proof includes `have h := x.isLt` followed by `omega`.
**Exploit**: `omega` alone works because it automatically reads `isLt` from the type context.
**Impact**: Low. The `have h := x.isLt` step is pedagogical scaffolding that makes the bound visible to the learner. The LESSON (that isLt exists and provides the bound) is conveyed through the intro/conclusion text regardless. And the scaffolding extraction pattern IS the standard approach for readability.

---

## 6. Coverage Gaps

None significant for the implemented worlds. All core MeetFin and FinNavigation items from the coverage map have intro + support + retrieval/integration in boss.

Minor note: The coverage map lists `← rw` (reverse rewriting) as a separate skill. Neither world explicitly teaches `← rw`. It's not needed in these worlds, but should be introduced before it's required later. (This is a course-level coverage note, not a defect in these worlds.)

---

## 7. Granularity Defects

None. Both worlds are well-scoped:
- **MeetFin** (20 levels): Each level has one dominant lesson. The progression from construction (L01) through extraction (L02-L04) to equality reasoning (L05-L10) to boundary cases (L11-L14) to case analysis (L15-L17) to inequality/existentials (L18-L19) to boss (L20) is coherent.
- **FinNavigation** (9 levels): Tight focus on three navigation functions. Introduction (L01-L04) → combination (L05) → separation fact (L06) → recognition patterns (L07-L08) → boss (L09).

No levels are overbroad or overfine.

---

## 8. Learner Simulation Notes

### Attentive Novice

**MeetFin**:
- **First stuck point**: L15 (destructuring syntax is new) or L16 (nested case analysis with multiple branches). Both have thorough hints.
- **Most likely wrong move**: In L16, trying `cases x` without the `with | mk v hlt =>` syntax. The hint addresses this.
- **Hint rescue quality**: Excellent. Every level has at least one visible hint with strategy guidance, plus hidden hints for specific syntax. The boss has hints at every case branch.
- **Lesson legibility**: Strong. Intro + hints + conclusion form a coherent narrative at each level. The summary tables in conclusions (L02, L08, L16, L20) consolidate learning well.

**FinNavigation**:
- **First stuck point**: L08 (constructing the castSucc preimage requires a creative step: `use ⟨i.val, h⟩`). This is the first level where the learner must *invent* a witness rather than follow a recipe.
- **Hint rescue quality**: Good. The creative step in L08 is covered by the hint.

### Experienced Lean User

- Would want `fin_cases` (disabled, correctly). Conclusions explain why and when it'll be unlocked.
- Might use `rfl` on L01/L03/L04 of FinNavigation. Acknowledged, low risk.
- Would find L04 of MeetFin (rfl) trivial but the point about definitional equality is well-made.
- No avoidable friction or inventory clutter.

---

## 9. Boss Integrity Notes

### MeetFin Boss (L20)

**Structure**: Conjunction of two parts.
- **Part 1** (transitivity through extensionality): Requires `constructor`, `intro`, `ext`, `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`, `omega`. Integrates L05/L07/L14 moves.
- **Part 2** (non-triviality of Fin 3): Requires destructuring, nested case analysis (3 valid + 1 impossible), `use ⟨k, by omega⟩`, `intro h; cases h`, `absurd`. Integrates L15-L19 moves.

**Seeded subskills**: All moves used in boss were taught in prior levels. No untaught micro-skills.
**Integration quality**: Both parts require combining multiple moves. Part 2 especially demands planning (which witness to choose for each case).
**Surprise burden**: Part 2 is longer than any individual practice level (L16 or L17), but the case analysis pattern is identical — just applied to a different goal shape. Acceptable.
**Can learner explain why?**: Yes — Part 1 is "Fin equality is determined by values, and value equality is transitive." Part 2 is "Fin 3 has at least 2 distinct elements, proved by exhibiting a different element for each case."

**Verdict**: Well-designed boss. Not a fake boss (genuine integration, not concatenation). Not a lottery boss (all moves seeded).

### FinNavigation Boss (L09)

**Structure**: Conjunction of two parts.
- **Part 1** (decomposition): Long case analysis where each branch uses either "recognize as last" (L07 pattern) or "find castSucc preimage" (L08 pattern). Requires destructuring from MeetFin.
- **Part 2** (succ injectivity): `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h; ext; omega`. Combines ext_iff, val_succ, ext, omega.

**Seeded subskills**: All seeded. Part 1 uses L07 + L08 patterns within MeetFin case analysis. Part 2 uses L03-L05 val lemma patterns.
**Integration quality**: Part 1 is genuine integration — case analysis (from MeetFin) combined with FinNavigation recognition patterns. Part 2 is a clean application of the rewrite-to-values pattern.
**Disabled theorems**: `Fin.succ_inj` and `Fin.forall_fin_two` correctly disabled to prevent one-shots.

**Verdict**: Well-designed boss. Both parts integrate world moves. The decomposition is the structural heart of Fin navigation.

---

## 10. Technical Risks

1. **Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic import scope**: Both worlds import only `Game.Metadata` which imports `Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic`. This provides the full Fin API. Some Fin lemmas not in the disabled list could theoretically close levels (e.g., `Fin.eq_zero` for L14). Practically low risk since novice learners won't know these lemma names.

2. **Future omega power creep**: If a future Lean version makes omega smarter about unfolding Fin definitions (currently it treats `Fin.last`, `castSucc`, `succ` as opaque), the FinNavigation val lemma lessons could become bypassable. Currently safe.

3. **Translation warnings**: Build log shows "No translation (en) found" warnings for all TacticDoc entries. These are cosmetic GameServer warnings, not functional issues. They'll produce missing translations in the i18n system but won't affect English-language gameplay.

---

## 11. Ranked Patch List

1. **[P0] Fix MeetFin L15 statement** to require destructuring. Recommended: change statement to `∃ (v : ℕ), v < 3 ∧ x = ⟨v, by omega⟩` or similar that omega cannot bypass. Update proof and hints accordingly.

2. **[P2] Add `rfl` acknowledgment** to FinNavigation L03/L04 conclusions (matching L01's existing note about definitional transparency).

3. **[P2] Consider adding ext practice level** between MeetFin L14 and L20 if enrichment pass occurs.

---

## 12. What to Playtest Again After Patching

1. **MeetFin L15** (after statement change) — verify the new statement forces destructuring and the hints guide the new proof correctly.
2. **MeetFin L16** — verify it still works as the next step after the revised L15.
3. **MeetFin Boss L20** — verify Part 2 still functions correctly with any L15 changes.

World "MeetFin"
Title "Meet Fin"

Introduction "
# Meet Fin

Welcome to the world of finite types!

In mathematics, finite index sets like $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ appear
everywhere — as indices of vectors, as labels for vertices of a polygon,
as positions in a sequence. In Lean 4, this finite index set is called
`Fin n`.

An element of `Fin n` is not just a number — it's a number **bundled
with a proof** that it's less than `n`. This bundling is what makes
`Fin n` a proper type rather than just a naming convention. The
advantage: the type system enforces the bound for you, so out-of-range
indices are impossible to construct.

`Fin n` is a *subtype* of `ℕ` — the subtype `{ k : ℕ // k < n }`.
You'll see this subtype pattern again in later courses.

In this world, you'll learn to:
- **Construct** elements of `Fin n`
- **Extract** their natural number values
- **Prove** when two `Fin` elements are equal (or not!)
- **Handle** the edge cases `Fin 0` (empty!) and `Fin 1` (singleton)
- **Case-split** to reason about all elements of a small `Fin` type

Three warnings before we begin:
1. `Fin n` starts at **0**, not 1. The elements are $0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$.
   (Why? Because the defining predicate is `k < n` on `ℕ`, which
   naturally gives $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$.)
2. `Fin 0` is **empty**. There is no natural number less than 0.
3. Two `Fin` elements are equal when their **values** are equal — you
   need `Fin.ext_iff` (or the `ext` tactic) to access this fact.

The boss at the end will ask you to combine all these moves in a single
proof. Let's begin!
"

import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L01_TheLastElement
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L02_LastIsMaximum
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L03_EmbeddingCastSucc
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L04_MovingForwardSucc
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L05_CastSuccBelowSucc
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L06_CastSuccNeverLast
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L07_RecognizingLast
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L08_FindingCastSucc
import Game.Levels.FinNavigation.L09_Boss

World "FinNavigation"
Title "Fin Navigation"

Introduction "
# Navigating Fin

You've learned to construct and compare elements of `Fin n`. Now
you'll learn to *navigate* within `Fin n` — accessing specific
landmark elements and moving between `Fin n` and `Fin (n+1)`.

Three functions form the navigation toolkit:

**`Fin.last n`** — the maximum element of `Fin (n+1)`, with
value `n`. It's the boundary element, sitting at the top.

**`Fin.castSucc`** — embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n+1)` by keeping
the same value but relaxing the bound. If `i : Fin n` has value
`k`, then `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` also has value `k`.

**`Fin.succ`** — moves forward: if `i : Fin n` has value `k`,
then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has value `k + 1`.

Both `castSucc` and `succ` send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` — they
increase the type's size by 1 — but they do different things to
the value: `castSucc` preserves it, `succ` increments it.

The central insight of this world: every element of `Fin (n+1)`
is either
- `Fin.last n` (the maximum), or
- `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n` (an embedded smaller element)

This decomposition is the `Fin` version of *every natural number
$k \\leq n$ either equals $n$ or is strictly less than $n$*.
It will be essential in later worlds when you build and decompose
tuples.

In this world, you'll learn to:
- Compute the value of `last`, `castSucc`, and `succ` using their
  val lemmas
- Prove that `castSucc` never reaches `last`
- Recognize whether an element is `last` or a `castSucc` image
- Combine all of this to prove the decomposition
"

    13→**destructuring** with `cases`.
    14→
    15→When you write:
    16→```
    17→cases x with | mk v hlt =>
    18→```

    21→- `hlt : v < 3` — the bound proof
    22→

    24→standalone variables in your context.
    25→

    27→you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** — asking whether
    28→`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
    29→variable, which destructuring provides.
    30→

    32→

    34→Destructure first, then use `omega` to bridge the gap.
    35→"

    40→  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
    41→  cases x with
    42→  | mk v hlt =>

    44→    `v ≤ 2`. Since `v < 3` implies `v ≤ 2`, `omega` handles this."

    49→

    51→a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
    52→apart with `cases` to expose its components.

    55→|---|---|

    57→| `x.val` | `v` |
    58→| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |

    61→`v ≤ 2` is equivalent but not syntactically identical — `omega`

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 15

Title "Destructuring Fin"

Introduction "
# Destructuring Fin Elements

So far, you've accessed the parts of a `Fin n` element using dot
notation: `x.val` and `x.isLt`. There's another way:
**destructuring** with `cases`.

When you write:
```
cases x with | mk v hlt =>
```
Lean replaces `x : Fin 3` with two new hypotheses:
- `v : ℕ` — the underlying value
- `hlt : v < 3` — the bound proof

This is the same information as `x.val` and `x.isLt`, but now they're
standalone variables in your context.

Why learn a second way to access these? Because in the next level,
you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** — asking whether
`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
variable, which destructuring provides.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` has value at most 2.

Note: `.isLt` gives `x.val < 3`, but the goal asks for `x.val ≤ 2`.
Destructure first, then use `omega` to bridge the gap.
"

/-- After destructuring, omega can derive the bound in a different form. -/
Statement (x : Fin 3) : x.val ≤ 2 := by
  Hint "Destructure `x` into its components:
  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  cases x with
  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Now you have `v : ℕ` and `hlt : v < 3`. The goal is
    `v ≤ 2`. Since `v < 3` implies `v ≤ 2`, `omega` handles this."
    omega

Conclusion "
You've just destructured a `Fin` element for the first time.

The pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` is the `Fin` version of
a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
apart with `cases` to expose its components.

| Before destructuring | After |
|---|---|
| `x : Fin 3` | `v : ℕ`, `hlt : v < 3` |
| `x.val` | `v` |
| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |

After destructuring, `hlt` gives the raw bound `v < 3`. The goal
`v ≤ 2` is equivalent but not syntactically identical — `omega`
handles the conversion. You'll need destructuring in the next level
for full case analysis.
"

1→import Game.Metadata
     2→
     3→World "MeetFin"
     4→Level 9
     5→
     6→Title "The Round-Trip Property"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# Taking Apart and Reassembling
    10→
    11→In Level 1, you *constructed* a `Fin` element with `⟨k, by omega⟩`.
    12→In Level 2, you *extracted* its parts with `.val` and `.isLt`.
    13→
    14→A natural question: if you know an element's value, can you identify
    15→it with an explicit `⟨k, proof⟩` term?
    16→
    17→Here you have `i : Fin 5` and a hypothesis `h : i.val = 3`. The
    18→goal asks you to show `i = ⟨3, by omega⟩` — that `i` is the
    19→element with value 3.
    20→
    21→**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality
    22→to a value equality, then use `h` to close.
    23→"
    24→
    25→/-- If a Fin element has value 3, it equals the explicit construction ⟨3, _⟩. -/
    26→Statement (i : Fin 5) (h : i.val = 3) : i = ⟨3, by omega⟩ := by
    27→  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to convert the `Fin` equality to a
    28→  value equality."
    29→  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
    30→  Hint "The goal is now `i.val = 3`, which is exactly `{h}`."
    31→  exact h
    32→
    33→Conclusion "
    34→The round-trip principle: if you know what value a `Fin` element
    35→carries, you can identify it with the explicit construction.
    36→
    37→This is `Fin.ext_iff` in action — equality of `Fin` elements reduces
    38→to equality of their values, and once the values match, the elements
    39→are the same.
    40→"
    41→

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 9

Title "The Round-Trip Property"

Introduction "
# Taking Apart and Reassembling

In Level 1, you *constructed* a `Fin` element with `⟨k, by omega⟩`.
In Level 2, you *extracted* its parts with `.val` and `.isLt`.

A natural question: if you know an element's value, can you identify
it with an explicit `⟨k, proof⟩` term?

Here you have `i : Fin 5` and a hypothesis `h : i.val = 3`. The
goal asks you to show `i = ⟨3, by omega⟩` — that `i` is the
element with value 3.

**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality
to a value equality, then use `h` to close.
"

/-- If a Fin element has value 3, it equals the explicit construction ⟨3, _⟩. -/
Statement (i : Fin 5) (h : i.val = 3) : i = ⟨3, by omega⟩ := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to convert the `Fin` equality to a
  value equality."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
  Hint "The goal is now `i.val = 3`, which is exactly `{h}`."
  exact h

Conclusion "
The round-trip principle: if you know what value a `Fin` element
carries, you can identify it with the explicit construction.

This is `Fin.ext_iff` in action — equality of `Fin` elements reduces
to equality of their values, and once the values match, the elements
are the same.
"

content.content:
1→import Game.Metadata
     2→
     3→World "MeetFin"
     4→Level 10
     5→
     6→Title "Only the Value Matters"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# Proof Irrelevance for Fin
    10→
    11→A `Fin n` element bundles a value with a *proof* that the value is
    12→in range. But what if two elements have the same value? Are they
    13→the same element?
    14→
    15→The answer: **only the value matters**. Two `Fin` elements with the
    16→same underlying natural number are equal, regardless of how their
    17→bound proofs were obtained. This follows directly from `Fin.ext_iff`:
    18→equality depends only on `.val`.
    19→
    20→Here you have two elements `a` and `b` of `Fin 5`, and you know

    22→they must be equal.
    23→
    24→In practice, you might get bound proofs from different sources —
    25→one from a hypothesis, one from `omega`, one from a lemma. Proof
    26→irrelevance means you never need to worry about *which* proof was
    27→used.
    28→
    29→**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce to a value equality,
    30→then use `omega` to close the arithmetic (since `ha` and `hb`
    31→together imply `a.val = b.val`).
    32→"
    33→
    34→/-- Fin elements with the same value are equal, regardless of the bound proof. -/
    35→Statement (a b : Fin 5) (ha : a.val = 3) (hb : b.val = 3) : a = b := by
    36→  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality to a
    37→  value equality."
    38→  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
    39→  Hint "Now the goal is `a.val = b.val`. You know `{ha}` and
    40→  `{hb}` — both values are `3`. `omega` can close this."
    41→  omega

    45→`Fin` element depends only on its value, not on which proof
    46→witnesses the bound.
    47→

    49→two-step combo — **reduce to values, then compute** — is the
    50→standard way to prove `Fin` equalities when the values can be
    51→determined from hypotheses. We'll call this the **Fin equality
    52→pattern**: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`. You'll use it frequently.
    53→
    54→This isn't special to `Fin` — it applies to **every subtype** in Lean.
    55→Any type of the form `{ x : α // P x }` (a value bundled with a
    56→proof) has the same property: two elements are equal when their values
    57→are equal, regardless of the proofs. You'll meet other subtypes later
    58→(like `{ n : ℕ // n.Prime }`), and proof irrelevance will apply there
    59→too.
    60→"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 10

Title "Only the Value Matters"

Introduction "
# Proof Irrelevance for Fin

A `Fin n` element bundles a value with a *proof* that the value is
in range. But what if two elements have the same value? Are they
the same element?

The answer: **only the value matters**. Two `Fin` elements with the
same underlying natural number are equal, regardless of how their
bound proofs were obtained. This follows directly from `Fin.ext_iff`:
equality depends only on `.val`.

Here you have two elements `a` and `b` of `Fin 5`, and you know
`ha : a.val = 3` and `hb : b.val = 3`. Since both have value `3`,
they must be equal.

In practice, you might get bound proofs from different sources —
one from a hypothesis, one from `omega`, one from a lemma. Proof
irrelevance means you never need to worry about *which* proof was
used.

**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce to a value equality,
then use `omega` to close the arithmetic (since `ha` and `hb`
together imply `a.val = b.val`).
"

/-- Fin elements with the same value are equal, regardless of the bound proof. -/
Statement (a b : Fin 5) (ha : a.val = 3) (hb : b.val = 3) : a = b := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality to a
  value equality."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
  Hint "Now the goal is `a.val = b.val`. You know `{ha}` and
  `{hb}` — both values are `3`. `omega` can close this."
  omega

Conclusion "
This property is called **proof irrelevance**: the identity of a
`Fin` element depends only on its value, not on which proof
witnesses the bound.

Notice the proof pattern: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` then `omega`. This
two-step combo — **reduce to values, then compute** — is the
standard way to prove `Fin` equalities when the values can be
determined from hypotheses. We'll call this the **Fin equality
pattern**: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`. You'll use it frequently.

This isn't special to `Fin` — it applies to **every subtype** in Lean.
Any type of the form `{ x : α // P x }` (a value bundled with a
proof) has the same property: two elements are equal when their values
are equal, regardless of the proofs. You'll meet other subtypes later
(like `{ n : ℕ // n.Prime }`), and proof irrelevance will apply there
too.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 18

Title "Proving Inequality"

Introduction "
# Proving Two Fin Elements Are Not Equal

In Level 16, you used `absurd` when a bound was violated — the value
was too large but the bound said it must be small. Here the situation
is different: the *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`),
and a simpler pattern handles this case.

How do you prove `a ≠ b` for `Fin` elements? In Lean, `a ≠ b` is
defined as `a = b → False` — a function that takes an equality proof
and derives a contradiction.

The strategy is:
1. `intro h` — assume `a = b` (giving `h : a = b`)
2. `cases h` — Lean checks if the constructors match; if the underlying
   values differ, it closes the goal automatically

When `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩` and `a ≠ b` as natural numbers, `cases h`
performs *injection* — it sees the values inside the constructors don't
match and derives a contradiction. This is the same idea as `Fin.ext_iff`
in reverse: if the values differ, the elements can't be equal.

**Your task**: Prove that `0` and `1` are different elements of `Fin 3`.
"

/-- `0` and `1` are distinct elements of `Fin 3`. -/
Statement : (0 : Fin 3) ≠ 1 := by
  Hint "Remember, `≠` means `= → False`. Start with `intro h`
  to assume they are equal."
  intro h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says these `Fin` elements are equal. But their
  underlying values are `0` and `1` — different natural numbers.
  `cases h` performs injection and sees the contradiction."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h`."
  cases h

Conclusion "
The pattern for proving `a ≠ b` on `Fin` elements is:
```
intro h
cases h
```

This works because `cases` on an equality of constructors checks
whether the inner values match. When they don't (like `0 ≠ 1` at the
`ℕ` level), Lean derives a contradiction automatically.

You could also prove this using `Fin.ext_iff`: `intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`.
This reduces the `Fin` equality to `0 = 1` at the `ℕ` level, then
`omega` sees the contradiction. Both approaches work — `cases h` is
more direct, while `ext_iff + omega` reuses the Fin equality pattern.

This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

1→import Game.Metadata
     2→
     3→World "MeetFin"
     4→Level 1
     5→
     6→Title "What is Fin n?"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# What is `Fin n`?
    10→
    11→In mathematics, we often work with finite index sets like
    12→$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ — the first $n$ natural numbers.
    13→
    14→In Lean 4, this is captured by the type `Fin n`. An element of `Fin n`
    15→is a natural number `k` **together with a proof** that `k < n`.
    16→
    17→More precisely:
    18→$$\\texttt{Fin } n = \\{ k : \\mathbb{N} \\mid k < n \\}$$
    19→
    20→**Why not just use `ℕ`?** If you index a length-5 vector with a plain
    21→natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` — the type checker
    22→can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the
    23→type: you literally *cannot construct* an element unless you prove it's
    24→in range. The type system enforces the constraint for you.
    25→
    26→This is an example of a *subtype* — a type carved out of a larger type
    27→by a predicate. `Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : ℕ // k < n }`.
    28→
    29→To construct an element of `Fin n`, use the **anonymous constructor**
    30→`⟨k, proof⟩`, where:
    31→- `k` is the natural number
    32→- `proof` shows `k < n`
    33→
    34→For the proof part, the `omega` tactic handles routine arithmetic
    35→goals like `3 < 5`.
    36→
    37→**Your task**: Construct any element of `Fin 5`. There are five to
    38→choose from: $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.
    39→
    40→Try `exact ⟨3, by omega⟩`.
    41→
    42→*Notation*: type `⟨` as `\\langle` and `⟩` as `\\rangle`.
    43→"
    44→
    45→/-- Constructs a specific element of `Fin 5`. -/
    46→Statement : Fin 5 := by
    47→  Hint "Use the anonymous constructor `⟨k, by omega⟩` where `k` is any
    48→  natural number less than 5. For example, `exact ⟨3, by omega⟩`."
    49→  exact ⟨3, by omega⟩
    50→
    51→Conclusion "
    52→Well done! You just constructed the element $3$ as a term of type
    53→`Fin 5`.
    54→
    55→The key pattern: to build a `Fin n`, you provide **two things** — a
    56→number and a proof that it's in range. The `⟨k, by omega⟩` idiom
    57→will appear throughout this world.
    58→
    59→In ordinary mathematics, saying *let $x = 3 \\in \\{0, \\ldots, 4\\}$*
    60→is informal. In Lean, you must **prove** the element is in range.
    61→That's what the second component does.
    62→
    63→*Note*: `exact 3` also works here because Lean can automatically coerce
    64→a numeric literal into `Fin 5` when the bound is known. But in later
    65→worlds where the bound is a variable, you'll need the explicit
    66→constructor `⟨k, proof⟩`.
    67→"
    68→
    69→/-- `omega` closes goals involving linear arithmetic over `ℕ` and `ℤ`.
    70→It handles equalities, inequalities, and combinations.
    71→
    72→## Syntax
    73→```
    74→omega
    75→```
    76→
    77→## When to use it
    78→When the goal (or a contradiction in the hypotheses) follows from
    79→linear arithmetic over natural numbers or integers.
    80→
    81→## Example
    82→```
    83→-- Goal: 3 < 5
    84→omega
    85→```
    86→
    87→## Warning
    88→`omega` only handles *linear* arithmetic. It cannot solve goals
    89→involving multiplication of variables (like `n * m < n * n + 1`).
    90→-/
    91→TacticDoc omega
    92→
    93→/-- `Fin n` is the type of natural numbers strictly less than `n`.
    94→
    95→An element of `Fin n` consists of:
    96→- `val : ℕ` — the underlying natural number
    97→- `isLt : val < n` — a proof that the number is in range
    98→
    99→`Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : ℕ // k < n }`.
   100→
   101→## Construction
   102→Use the anonymous constructor: `⟨k, proof⟩`
   103→
   104→## Example
   105→`⟨3, by omega⟩ : Fin 5` — the number 3 with a proof that `3 < 5`.
   106→
   107→## Warning
   108→`Fin n` has elements `0, 1, ..., n-1` (NOT `1, ..., n`).
   109→`Fin 0` is **empty** — there is no natural number less than 0.
   110→-/
   111→DefinitionDoc Fin as "Fin"
   112→
   113→/-- `Fin.mk val isLt` constructs an element of `Fin n` from a natural
   114→number `val` and a proof `isLt : val < n`.
   115→
   116→Equivalently, use the anonymous constructor `⟨val, isLt⟩`.
   117→
   118→## Example
   119→`⟨3, by omega⟩ : Fin 5`
   120→-/
   121→DefinitionDoc Fin.mk as "Fin.mk"
   122→
   123→NewTactic omega exact rw intro cases «have» use left right rfl constructor
   124→NewDefinition Fin Fin.mk
   125→
   126→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases
   127→

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 1

Title "What is Fin n?"

Introduction "
# What is `Fin n`?

In mathematics, we often work with finite index sets like
$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ — the first $n$ natural numbers.

In Lean 4, this is captured by the type `Fin n`. An element of `Fin n`
is a natural number `k` **together with a proof** that `k < n`.

More precisely:
$$\\texttt{Fin } n = \\{ k : \\mathbb{N} \\mid k < n \\}$$

**Why not just use `ℕ`?** If you index a length-5 vector with a plain
natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` — the type checker
can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the
type: you literally *cannot construct* an element unless you prove it's
in range. The type system enforces the constraint for you.

This is an example of a *subtype* — a type carved out of a larger type
by a predicate. `Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : ℕ // k < n }`.

To construct an element of `Fin n`, use the **anonymous constructor**
`⟨k, proof⟩`, where:
- `k` is the natural number
- `proof` shows `k < n`

For the proof part, the `omega` tactic handles routine arithmetic
goals like `3 < 5`.

**Your task**: Construct any element of `Fin 5`. There are five to
choose from: $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

Try `exact ⟨3, by omega⟩`.

*Notation*: type `⟨` as `\\langle` and `⟩` as `\\rangle`.
"

/-- Constructs a specific element of `Fin 5`. -/
Statement : Fin 5 := by
  Hint "Use the anonymous constructor `⟨k, by omega⟩` where `k` is any
  natural number less than 5. For example, `exact ⟨3, by omega⟩`."
  exact ⟨3, by omega⟩

Conclusion "
Well done! You just constructed the element $3$ as a term of type
`Fin 5`.

The key pattern: to build a `Fin n`, you provide **two things** — a
number and a proof that it's in range. The `⟨k, by omega⟩` idiom
will appear throughout this world.

In ordinary mathematics, saying *let $x = 3 \\in \\{0, \\ldots, 4\\}$*
is informal. In Lean, you must **prove** the element is in range.
That's what the second component does.

*Note*: `exact 3` also works here because Lean can automatically coerce
a numeric literal into `Fin 5` when the bound is known. But in later
worlds where the bound is a variable, you'll need the explicit
constructor `⟨k, proof⟩`.
"

/-- `omega` closes goals involving linear arithmetic over `ℕ` and `ℤ`.
It handles equalities, inequalities, and combinations.

## Syntax
```
omega
```

## When to use it
When the goal (or a contradiction in the hypotheses) follows from
linear arithmetic over natural numbers or integers.

## Example
```
-- Goal: 3 < 5
omega
```

## Warning
`omega` only handles *linear* arithmetic. It cannot solve goals
involving multiplication of variables (like `n * m < n * n + 1`).
-/
TacticDoc omega

/-- `Fin n` is the type of natural numbers strictly less than `n`.

An element of `Fin n` consists of:
- `val : ℕ` — the underlying natural number
- `isLt : val < n` — a proof that the number is in range

`Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : ℕ // k < n }`.

## Construction
Use the anonymous constructor: `⟨k, proof⟩`

## Example
`⟨3, by omega⟩ : Fin 5` — the number 3 with a proof that `3 < 5`.

## Warning
`Fin n` has elements `0, 1, ..., n-1` (NOT `1, ..., n`).
`Fin 0` is **empty** — there is no natural number less than 0.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin as "Fin"

/-- `Fin.mk val isLt` constructs an element of `Fin n` from a natural
number `val` and a proof `isLt : val < n`.

Equivalently, use the anonymous constructor `⟨val, isLt⟩`.

## Example
`⟨3, by omega⟩ : Fin 5`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.mk as "Fin.mk"

NewTactic omega exact rw intro cases «have» use left right rfl constructor
NewDefinition Fin Fin.mk

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

1→import Game.Metadata
     2→
     3→World "FinNavigation"
     4→Level 3
     5→
     6→Title "Embedding with castSucc"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type
    10→
    11→What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?
    12→
    13→**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
    14→returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.
    15→
    16→For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
    17→`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
    18→change — only the type's upper bound increases.
    19→
    20→The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
    21→$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$
    22→
    23→Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
    24→So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.
    25→
    26→**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
    27→"
    28→
    29→/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
    30→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
    31→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
    32→  intro i
    33→  exact Fin.val_castSucc i
    34→
    35→Conclusion "
    36→`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
    37→`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
    38→*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
    39→type now has room for one more element at the top.
    40→
    41→The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
    42→(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
    43→- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
    44→- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)
    45→
    46→You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.
    47→
    48→In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
    49→\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
    50→"
    51→
    52→/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
    53→the same value but relaxing the upper bound.
    54→
    55→## Syntax
    56→```
    57→i.castSucc      -- dot notation
    58→Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
    59→```
    60→
    61→## Type
    62→`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
    63→
    64→## Value
    65→`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)
    66→
    67→## When to use it
    68→When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
    69→`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
    70→-/
    71→DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"
    72→
    73→/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.
    74→
    75→## Syntax
    76→```
    77→rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
    78→exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
    79→```
    80→
    81→## When to use it
    82→When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
    83→-/
    84→TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"
    85→
    86→NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
    87→NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc
    88→
    89→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
    90→

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 3

Title "Embedding with castSucc"

Introduction "
# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type

What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?

**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.

For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
change — only the type's upper bound increases.

The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$

Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.

**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
"

/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
  intro i
  exact Fin.val_castSucc i

Conclusion "
`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
type now has room for one more element at the top.

The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)

You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.

In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
"

/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
the same value but relaxing the upper bound.

## Syntax
```
i.castSucc      -- dot notation
Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
```

## Type
`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`

## Value
`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)

## When to use it
When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"

/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 4

Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"

Introduction "
# Fin.succ: The Next Element

There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.

If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
value `k + 1`.

Compare with `castSucc`:
- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)

Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
forward by one.

The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$

**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
"

/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
  intro i
  exact Fin.val_succ i

Conclusion "
You now have the three navigation functions and their val lemmas:

| Function | Type | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.last n` | `Fin (n+1)` | `n` |
| `i.castSucc` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val` (unchanged) |
| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |

The val lemmas (`val_last`, `val_castSucc`, `val_succ`) convert
`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving
`Fin` facts is: **rewrite to values, then omega**.

In the next level, you'll combine these val lemmas to prove a
relationship between `castSucc` and `succ`.
"

/-- `Fin.succ` maps `Fin n` to `Fin (n + 1)` by adding 1 to the
underlying value.

## Syntax
```
i.succ      -- dot notation
Fin.succ i  -- explicit
```

## Type
`Fin.succ : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`

## Value
`i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (see `Fin.val_succ`)

## Comparison with castSucc
- `castSucc`: same value, bigger type
- `succ`: value + 1, bigger type
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.succ as "Fin.succ"

/-- `Fin.val_succ i` states that `i.succ.val = i.val + 1`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_succ]      -- rewrites i.succ.val to i.val + 1
exact Fin.val_succ i   -- closes the goal directly
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.succ` to the underlying value.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_succ as "Fin.val_succ" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.succ
NewTheorem Fin.val_succ

1→import Game.Metadata
     2→
     3→World "FinNavigation"
     4→Level 9
     5→
     6→Title "Boss: The Decomposition"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# Boss: Putting Navigation Together
    10→
    11→Time to combine everything. This boss has two parts.
    12→
    13→**Part 1 — The Decomposition**: Every element of `Fin 3` is
    14→either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin 2`.
    15→
    16→This is the structural heart of `Fin` navigation: `Fin (n+1)`
    17→splits into the `castSucc` images (from `Fin n`) and
    18→`Fin.last n`. No element falls outside these two cases.
    19→
    20→Strategy: destructure (Level 15 of MeetFin), case-split on
    21→the value, then use Level 7 (recognizing last) or Level 8
    22→(finding castSucc) for each case.
    23→
    24→**Part 2 — Succ is injective**: If `i.succ = j.succ`, then
    25→`i = j`. This uses `ext`/`ext_iff` and `val_succ` to reduce
    26→to arithmetic.
    27→"
    28→
    29→/-- The last-or-castSucc decomposition, and succ injectivity. -/
    30→Statement : (∀ i : Fin 3, i = Fin.last 2 ∨ ∃ j : Fin 2, i = j.castSucc) ∧
    31→            (∀ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ → i = j) := by
    32→  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
    33→  constructor
    34→  -- Part 1: decomposition
    35→  · Hint "Part 1: prove every `Fin 3` element is `last` or `castSucc`.
    36→    Start with `intro i`, then destructure `i` with
    37→    `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
    38→    intro i
    39→    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure: `cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
    40→    cases i with
    41→    | mk v hv =>
    42→      Hint "Now `v : ℕ` and `hv : v < 3`. Case-split on `v`:
    43→      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    44→      cases v with
    45→      | zero =>
    46→        Hint "Here `v = 0`. This is a `castSucc` image (value `< 2`).
    47→        Choose `right`, then provide the preimage `⟨0, by omega⟩ : Fin 2`,
    48→        then `ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`."
    49→        right
    50→        Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨0, by omega⟩`"
    51→        use ⟨0, by omega⟩
    52→        Hint (hidden := true) "`ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
    53→        ext
    54→        rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
    55→      | succ n =>
    56→        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`:
    57→        `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
    58→        cases n with
    59→        | zero =>
    60→          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 1`. Still a `castSucc` image.
    61→          `right; use ⟨1, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
    62→          right
    63→          use ⟨1, by omega⟩
    64→          ext
    65→          rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
    66→        | succ m =>
    67→          Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m` once more."
    68→          cases m with
    69→          | zero =>
    70→            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 2 = Fin.last 2`. Choose `left`,
    71→            then `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]`."
    72→            left
    73→            ext
    74→            rw [Fin.val_last]
    75→          | succ k =>
    76→            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
    77→            Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
    78→            exact absurd hv (by omega)
    79→  -- Part 2: succ injectivity
    80→  · Hint "Part 2: prove succ is injective. Start with `intro i j h`."
    81→    intro i j h
    82→    Hint "Convert `{h}` to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
    83→    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
    84→    Hint "Simplify succ values: `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h`.
    85→    This gives `i.val + 1 = j.val + 1`."
    86→    rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h
    87→    Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, and close with `omega`."
    88→    ext
    89→    omega
    90→
    91→Conclusion "
    92→Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Navigation**!
    93→
    94→**The Decomposition** (Part 1): Every element of `Fin (n+1)` is
    95→either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`. This
    96→is the `Fin` analog of: every natural number $k \\leq n$ either
    97→equals $n$ or is strictly less than $n$.
    98→
    99→This decomposition is *the* structural tool for `Fin`. When you
   100→build tuples in the next world using `Fin.cons` and `Fin.snoc`,
   101→the decomposition is what makes the access lemmas work: the
   102→first element goes to `castSucc 0`, and the last element goes
   103→to `Fin.last`.
   104→
   105→**Succ Injectivity** (Part 2): The value reasoning pattern —
   106→convert to values, simplify, omega — handles injectivity
   107→automatically.
   108→
   109→Here's your navigation toolkit:
   110→
   111→| Tool | What it does | Val lemma |
   112→|---|---|---|
   113→| `Fin.last n` | Maximum element of `Fin (n+1)` | `Fin.val_last` |
   114→| `i.castSucc` | Embed into larger type (same value) | `Fin.val_castSucc` |
   115→| `i.succ` | Move forward by 1 | `Fin.val_succ` |
   116→
   117→And the proof recipes:
   118→
   119→| Recipe | Pattern |
   120→|---|---|
   121→| Compare `Fin` values | val lemmas + `omega` |
   122→| Prove `Fin` equality | `ext` + val lemmas |
   123→| Prove `Fin` inequality | `ext_iff at h` + val lemmas at `h` + `omega` |
   124→| Identify as `last` | `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]` |
   125→| Find `castSucc` preimage | `use ⟨i.val, h⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` |
   126→
   127→**Where this is going**: In the next world (FinTuples), you'll use
   128→`Fin n → α` to model tuples — ordered lists of fixed length.
   129→The `castSucc`/`last` decomposition is what powers `Fin.cons`
   130→(adding at the front) and `Fin.snoc` (adding at the back).
   131→"
   132→
   133→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   134→DisabledTheorem Fin.succ_inj Fin.forall_fin_two
   135→

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 9

Title "Boss: The Decomposition"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting Navigation Together

Time to combine everything. This boss has two parts.

**Part 1 — The Decomposition**: Every element of `Fin 3` is
either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin 2`.

This is the structural heart of `Fin` navigation: `Fin (n+1)`
splits into the `castSucc` images (from `Fin n`) and
`Fin.last n`. No element falls outside these two cases.

Strategy: destructure (Level 15 of MeetFin), case-split on
the value, then use Level 7 (recognizing last) or Level 8
(finding castSucc) for each case.

**Part 2 — Succ is injective**: If `i.succ = j.succ`, then
`i = j`. This uses `ext`/`ext_iff` and `val_succ` to reduce
to arithmetic.
"

/-- The last-or-castSucc decomposition, and succ injectivity. -/
Statement : (∀ i : Fin 3, i = Fin.last 2 ∨ ∃ j : Fin 2, i = j.castSucc) ∧
            (∀ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ → i = j) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: decomposition
  · Hint "Part 1: prove every `Fin 3` element is `last` or `castSucc`.
    Start with `intro i`, then destructure `i` with
    `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
    intro i
    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure: `cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
    cases i with
    | mk v hv =>
      Hint "Now `v : ℕ` and `hv : v < 3`. Case-split on `v`:
      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
      cases v with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Here `v = 0`. This is a `castSucc` image (value `< 2`).
        Choose `right`, then provide the preimage `⟨0, by omega⟩ : Fin 2`,
        then `ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`."
        right
        Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨0, by omega⟩`"
        use ⟨0, by omega⟩
        Hint (hidden := true) "`ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
        ext
        rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
      | succ n =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`:
        `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
        cases n with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 1`. Still a `castSucc` image.
          `right; use ⟨1, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
          right
          use ⟨1, by omega⟩
          ext
          rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
        | succ m =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m` once more."
          cases m with
          | zero =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 2 = Fin.last 2`. Choose `left`,
            then `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]`."
            left
            ext
            rw [Fin.val_last]
          | succ k =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
            Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: succ injectivity
  · Hint "Part 2: prove succ is injective. Start with `intro i j h`."
    intro i j h
    Hint "Convert `{h}` to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
    Hint "Simplify succ values: `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h`.
    This gives `i.val + 1 = j.val + 1`."
    rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h
    Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, and close with `omega`."
    ext
    omega

Conclusion "
Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Navigation**!

**The Decomposition** (Part 1): Every element of `Fin (n+1)` is
either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`. This
is the `Fin` analog of: every natural number $k \\leq n$ either
equals $n$ or is strictly less than $n$.

This decomposition is *the* structural tool for `Fin`. When you
build tuples in the next world using `Fin.cons` and `Fin.snoc`,
the decomposition is what makes the access lemmas work: the
first element goes to `castSucc 0`, and the last element goes
to `Fin.last`.

**Succ Injectivity** (Part 2): The value reasoning pattern —
convert to values, simplify, omega — handles injectivity
automatically.

Here's your navigation toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Val lemma |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.last n` | Maximum element of `Fin (n+1)` | `Fin.val_last` |
| `i.castSucc` | Embed into larger type (same value) | `Fin.val_castSucc` |
| `i.succ` | Move forward by 1 | `Fin.val_succ` |

And the proof recipes:

| Recipe | Pattern |
|---|---|
| Compare `Fin` values | val lemmas + `omega` |
| Prove `Fin` equality | `ext` + val lemmas |
| Prove `Fin` inequality | `ext_iff at h` + val lemmas at `h` + `omega` |
| Identify as `last` | `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]` |
| Find `castSucc` preimage | `use ⟨i.val, h⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` |

**Where this is going**: In the next world (FinTuples), you'll use
`Fin n → α` to model tuples — ordered lists of fixed length.
The `castSucc`/`last` decomposition is what powers `Fin.cons`
(adding at the front) and `Fin.snoc` (adding at the back).
"
